title: Record producer

infobox.value: [Instrumental skills](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument"), [keyboard knowledge](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument"), [arranging](/wiki/Arranging "Arranging"), [vocal coaching](/wiki/Vocal_coach "Vocal coach")

[Music executive](/wiki/Music_executive "Music executive"), [recording engineer](/wiki/Audio_engineering "Audio engineering"), [executive producer](/wiki/Executive_producer "Executive producer"), [film producer](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer"), [A&R](/wiki/Artists_and_repertoire "Artists and repertoire")

or **music producer** is a music-creating project's overall supervisor whose responsibilities can involve a range of creative and technical leadership roles. Typically the job involves hands-on oversight of recording sessions; ensuring artists deliver acceptable and quality performances, supervising the technical engineering of the recording, and coordinating the production team and process. The producer's involvement in a musical project can vary in depth and scope. Sometimes in popular genres the producer may create the recording's entire sound and structure.[[1]](#cite_note-Moorefield-2010-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess201413-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3) However, in classical music recording, for example, the producer serves as more of a liaison between the conductor and the engineering team. The role is often likened to that of a [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), though there are important differences.[[1]](#cite_note-Moorefield-2010-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3) It is distinct from the role of an [executive producer](/wiki/Executive_producer "Executive producer"), who is mostly involved in the recording project on an administrative level, and from the [audio engineer](/wiki/Audio_engineer "Audio engineer") who operates the recording technology.

In the 1880s, the record industry began by simply having the artist perform at a [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph").[[14]](#cite_note-Thompson-2016-14) In 1924, the trade journal *[Talking Machine World](/wiki/Talking_Machine_World "Talking Machine World")*, covering the phonography and record industry, reported that Eddie King, [Victor Records](/wiki/Victor_Records "Victor Records")' manager of the "New York [artist and repertoire](/wiki/Artists_and_repertoire "Artists and repertoire") department", had planned a set of recordings in Los Angeles. Later, folklorist [Archie Green](/wiki/Archie_Green "Archie Green") called this perhaps the earliest printed use of *A&R man*. Actually, it says neither "A&R man" nor even "A&R", an initialism perhaps coined by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine in 1946, and entering wide use in the late 1940s.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber201841-15)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Individual supervising a musical project

"Musical production" redirects here. For musical production in the sense of a live performance involving music and choreography, see [Musical theatre](/wiki/Musical_theatre "Musical theatre").

A **record producer** or **music producer** is a music-creating project's overall supervisor whose responsibilities can involve a range of creative and technical leadership roles. Typically the job involves hands-on oversight of recording sessions; ensuring artists deliver acceptable and quality performances, supervising the technical engineering of the recording, and coordinating the production team and process. The producer's involvement in a musical project can vary in depth and scope. Sometimes in popular genres the producer may create the recording's entire sound and structure.[[1]](#cite_note-Moorefield-2010-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess201413-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3) However, in classical music recording, for example, the producer serves as more of a liaison between the conductor and the engineering team. The role is often likened to that of a [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), though there are important differences.[[1]](#cite_note-Moorefield-2010-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3) It is distinct from the role of an [executive producer](/wiki/Executive_producer "Executive producer"), who is mostly involved in the recording project on an administrative level, and from the [audio engineer](/wiki/Audio_engineer "Audio engineer") who operates the recording technology.

Varying by project, the producer may or may not choose all of the artists.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3) If employing only [synthesized](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") or [sampled](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") instrumentation, the producer may be the sole artist. Conversely, some artists do their own production.[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3) Some producers are their own engineers,[[5]](#cite_note-5) operating the technology across the project: preproduction, recording, [mixing](/wiki/Audio_mixing_(recorded_music) "Audio mixing (recorded music)"), and [mastering](/wiki/Audio_mastering "Audio mastering"). Record producers' precursors were "A&R men", who likewise could blend entrepreneurial, creative, and technical roles,[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess201413-2) but often exercised scant creative influence,[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber2018300-6) as record production still focused, into the 1950s, on simply improving the record's sonic match to the artists' own live performance.[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3)

Advances in recording technology, especially the 1940s advent of [tape recording](/wiki/Tape_recording "Tape recording")—which [Les Paul](/wiki/Les_Paul "Les Paul") promptly innovated further to develop [multitrack recording](/wiki/Multitrack_recording "Multitrack recording")[[7]](#cite_note-Hurtig-7)—and the 1950s rise of electronic instruments, turned record production into a specialty.[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3) In popular music, then, producers like [George Martin](/wiki/George_Martin "George Martin"), [Phil Spector](/wiki/Phil_Spector "Phil Spector") and [Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno") led its evolution into its present use of elaborate techniques and unrealistic sounds, creating songs impossible to originate live.[[1]](#cite_note-Moorefield-2010-1)[[8]](#cite_note-Kot-2016-8) After the 1980s, production's move from analog to digital further expanded possibilities.[[3]](#cite_note-Watson-2014-3) By now, DAWs, or [digital audio workstations](/wiki/Digital_audio_workstation "Digital audio workstation"), like [Logic Pro](/wiki/Logic_Pro "Logic Pro"), [Pro Tools](/wiki/Pro_Tools "Pro Tools") and [Studio One](/wiki/Studio_One_(software) "Studio One (software)"), turn an ordinary computer into a production console,[[9]](#cite_note-Kadis-2013-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Joseph-2016-10) whereby a solitary novice can become a skilled producer in a thrifty home studio.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess2013199-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Newman-2018-12) In the 2010s, efforts began to increase the prevalence of producers and engineers who are women, heavily outnumbered by men and prominently accoladed only in classical music.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess2013199-11)[[13]](#cite_note-Hertweck-2019-13)

## Production overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Production overview")]

As a broad project, the creation of a popular music recording may be split across three specialists: the [executive producer](/wiki/Music_executive "Music executive"), who oversees business partnerships and financing; the vocal producer or vocal arranger, who aids vocal performance via expert critique and coaching of vocal technique, and the record producer or music producer, who, often called simply the producer, directs the overall creative process of recording the song in its final mix.

The producer's roles can include gathering ideas, composing music, choosing [session musicians](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician"), proposing changes to song arrangements, coaching the performers, controlling sessions, supervising the [audio mixing](/wiki/Audio_mixing_(recorded_music) "Audio mixing (recorded music)"), and, in some cases, supervising the [audio mastering](/wiki/Audio_mastering "Audio mastering"). A producer may give creative control to the artists themselves, taking a supervisory or advisory role instead. As to qualifying for a [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy "Grammy") nomination, the [Recording Academy](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy") defines a producer:[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess201413-2)

The person who has overall creative and technical control of the entire recording project, and the individual recording sessions that are part of that project. He or she is present in the recording studio or at the location recording and works directly with the artist and engineer. The producer makes creative and aesthetic decisions that realize both the artist's and label's goals in the creation of musical content. Other duties include, but are not limited to: keeping budgets and schedules; adhering to deadlines; hiring musicians, singers, studios, and engineers; overseeing other staffing needs; and editing (Classical projects).

The producer often selects and collaborates with a mixing engineer, who focuses on the especially technological aspects of the recording process, namely, operating the electronic equipment and blending the raw, recorded tracks of the chosen performances, whether vocal or instrumental, into a *mix*, either stereo or surround sound. Then a mastering engineer further adjusts this recording for distribution on the chosen media. A producer may work on only one or two songs or on an artist's entire album, helping develop the album's overall vision. The record producers may also take on the role of executive producer, managing the budget, schedules, contracts, and negotiations.

## Historical developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Historical developments")]

### A&R team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: A&R team")]

**(Artists and Repertoires)**

In the 1880s, the record industry began by simply having the artist perform at a [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph").[[14]](#cite_note-Thompson-2016-14) In 1924, the trade journal *[Talking Machine World](/wiki/Talking_Machine_World "Talking Machine World")*, covering the phonography and record industry, reported that Eddie King, [Victor Records](/wiki/Victor_Records "Victor Records")' manager of the "New York [artist and repertoire](/wiki/Artists_and_repertoire "Artists and repertoire") department", had planned a set of recordings in Los Angeles. Later, folklorist [Archie Green](/wiki/Archie_Green "Archie Green") called this perhaps the earliest printed use of *A&R man*. Actually, it says neither "A&R man" nor even "A&R", an initialism perhaps coined by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine in 1946, and entering wide use in the late 1940s.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber201841-15)

In the 1920s and 1930s, A&R executives, like [Ben Selvin](/wiki/Ben_Selvin "Ben Selvin") at [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records"), [Nathaniel Shilkret](/wiki/Nathaniel_Shilkret "Nathaniel Shilkret") at Victor Records, and Bob Haring at [Brunswick Records](/wiki/Brunswick_Records "Brunswick Records") became the precursors of record producers, supervising recording and often leading session orchestras.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber2018300-6) During the 1940s, [major record labels](/wiki/Major_record_label "Major record label") increasingly opened official A&R departments, whose roles included supervision of recording.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber201841-15) Meanwhile, independent recording studios opened, helping originate *record producer* as a specialty.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] But despite a tradition of some A&R men writing music, *record production* still referred to just the manufacturing of record discs.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber2018300-6)

### Record producers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Record producers")]

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), pioneering A&R managers who transitioned influentially to record production as now understood, while sometimes owning independent labels, include [J. Mayo Williams](/wiki/J._Mayo_Williams "J. Mayo Williams") and [John Hammond](/wiki/John_Hammond_(record_producer) "John Hammond (record producer)"). Upon moving from Columbia Records to [Mercury Records](/wiki/Mercury_Records "Mercury Records"), Hammond appointed [Mitch Miller](/wiki/Mitch_Miller "Mitch Miller") to lead Mercury's popular recordings in New York. Miller then produced country-pop crossover hits by [Patti Page](/wiki/Patti_Page "Patti Page") and by [Frankie Laine](/wiki/Frankie_Laine "Frankie Laine"), moved from Mercury to Columbia, and became a leading A&R man of the 1950s.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber2018300-6)

During the decade, A&R executives increasingly directed songs' sonic signatures, although many still simply teamed singers with musicians, while yet others exercised virtually no creative influence. The term *record producer* in its current meaning—the creative director of song production—appearing in a 1953 issue of *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine, became widespread in the 1960s. Still, a formal distinction was elusive for some time more.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber2018300-6) A&R managers might still be creative directors, like [William "Mickey" Stevenson](/wiki/William_%22Mickey%22_Stevenson "William \"Mickey\" Stevenson"), hired by [Berry Gordy](/wiki/Berry_Gordy "Berry Gordy"), at the [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown") record label.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWardHuber2018283-16)

### Tape recording

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tape recording")]

In 1947, the American market gained audio recording onto magnetic tape.[[17]](#cite_note-17) At the record industry's 1880s dawn, rather, recording was done by [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph"), etching the sonic waveform vertically *into* a cylinder.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess201450-18) By the 1930s, a gramophone etched it laterally *across* a disc.[[19]](#cite_note-Philip-1998-19) Constrained in tonal range, whether bass or treble, and in [dynamic range](/wiki/Dynamic_range "Dynamic range"), records made a grand, concert piano sound like a small, upright piano, and maximal duration was four and a half minutes.[[14]](#cite_note-Thompson-2016-14)[[19]](#cite_note-Philip-1998-19) Selections and performance were often altered accordingly, and playing this disc—the wax master—destroyed it. The finality often caused anxiety that restrained performance to prevent error. In the 1940s, during World War II, the Germans refined audio recording onto magnetic tape—uncapping recording duration and allowing immediate playback, rerecording, and editing—a technology that premised emergence of record producers in their current roles.[[19]](#cite_note-Philip-1998-19)

### Multitrack recording

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Multitrack recording")]

Early in the recording industry, a record was attained by simply having all of the artists perform together live in one take.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess201450-18) In 1945,[[7]](#cite_note-Hurtig-7) by recording a musical element while playing a previously recorded record, [Les Paul](/wiki/Les_Paul "Les Paul") developed a recording technique called "sound on sound". By this, the final recording could be built piece by piece and tailored, effecting an editing process. In one case, Paul produced a song via 500 recorded discs. But, besides the tedium of this process, it serially degraded the sound quality of previously recorded elements, rerecorded as ambient sound. Yet in 1948, Paul adopted tape recording, enabling true multitrack recording by a new technique, "[overdubbing](/wiki/Overdubbing "Overdubbing")".[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess201450-18)

To enable overdubbing, Paul revised the tape recorder itself by adding a second playback head, and terming it the *preview head*. Joining the preexisting recording head, erase head, and playback head, the preview head allows the artist to hear the extant recording over headphones playing it in synchrony, "in sync", with the present performance being recorded alone on an isolated track.[[7]](#cite_note-Hurtig-7) This isolation of multiple tracks enables countless mixing possibilities. Producers began recording initially only the "bed tracks"—the [rhythm section](/wiki/Rhythm_section "Rhythm section"), including the [bassline](/wiki/Bassline "Bassline"), drums, and rhythm guitar—whereas vocals and instrument solos could be added later. A [horn section](/wiki/Horn_section "Horn section"), for example, could record a week later, and a [string section](/wiki/String_section "String section") another week later. A singer could perform her own backup vocals, or a guitarist could play 15 layers.

### Electronic instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Electronic instruments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Phil_Spector_with_MFQ_1965.png/250px-Phil_Spector_with_MFQ_1965.png)](/wiki/File:Phil_Spector_with_MFQ_1965.png)

[Phil Spector](/wiki/Phil_Spector "Phil Spector") producing [Modern Folk Quartet](/wiki/Modern_Folk_Quartet "Modern Folk Quartet"), 1966

Across the 1960s, popular music increasingly switched from acoustic instruments, like [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano"), [upright bass](/wiki/Upright_bass "Upright bass"), [acoustic guitar](/wiki/Acoustic_guitar "Acoustic guitar"), and [brass instruments](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument"), to electronic instruments, like [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar"), [keyboards](/wiki/Keyboard_(musical_instrument) "Keyboard (musical instrument)"), and [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer"), employing [instrument amplifiers](/wiki/Instrument_amplifier "Instrument amplifier") and speakers. These could mimic acoustic instruments or create utterly new sounds. Soon, by combining the capabilities of tape, [multitrack recording](/wiki/Multitrack_recording "Multitrack recording"), and electronic instruments, producers like [Phil Spector](/wiki/Phil_Spector "Phil Spector"), [George Martin](/wiki/George_Martin "George Martin"), and [Joe Meek](/wiki/Joe_Meek "Joe Meek") rendered sounds unattainable live.[[8]](#cite_note-Kot-2016-8) Similarly, in [jazz fusion](/wiki/Jazz_fusion "Jazz fusion"), [Teo Macero](/wiki/Teo_Macero "Teo Macero"), producing [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis")'s 1970 album *[Bitches Brew](/wiki/Bitches_Brew "Bitches Brew")*, spliced sections of extensive improvisation sessions.

[Brian Wilson](/wiki/Brian_Wilson "Brian Wilson") during a recording session, 1966

In the 1960s, rock acts like [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones"), and [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks") produced some of their own songs,[[20]](#cite_note-20) although many such songs are officially credited to specialist producers. Yet especially influential was [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys"), whose band leader [Brian Wilson](/wiki/Brian_Wilson "Brian Wilson") took over from his father [Murry](/wiki/Murry_Wilson "Murry Wilson") within a couple of years after the band's commercial breakthrough. By 1964, Wilson had taken Spector's techniques to unseen sophistication. Wilson alone produced all Beach Boys recordings between 1963 and 1967. Using multiple studios and multiple attempts of instrumental and vocal tracks, Wilson selected the best combinations of performance and audio quality, and used tape editing to assemble a composite performance.

### Digital production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Digital production")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

The 1980s advent of digital processes and formats rapidly replaced analog processes and formats, namely, tape and [vinyl](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record"). Although recording onto quality tape, at least half an inch wide and traveling 15 inches per second, had limited "tape hiss" to silent sections, digital's higher [signal-to-noise ratio](/wiki/Signal-to-noise_ratio "Signal-to-noise ratio"), SNR, abolished it. Digital also imparted to the music a perceived "pristine" sound quality, if also a loss of analog recordings' perceived "warm" quality and better-rounded bass.[[21]](#cite_note-Simmons-2006-21) Yet whereas editing tape media requires physically locating the target audio on the ribbon, cutting there, and splicing pieces, editing digital media offers inarguable advantages in ease, efficiency, and possibilities.

In the 1990s, digital production reached affordable home computers via production software. By now, recording and mixing are often centralized in DAWs, [digital audio workstations](/wiki/Digital_audio_workstation "Digital audio workstation")—for example, [Pro Tools](/wiki/Pro_Tools "Pro Tools"), [Logic Pro](/wiki/Logic_Pro "Logic Pro"), [Ableton](/wiki/Ableton_Live "Ableton Live"), [Cubase](/wiki/Steinberg_Cubase "Steinberg Cubase"), [Reason](/wiki/Reason_(software) "Reason (software)"), and [FL Studio](/wiki/FL_Studio "FL Studio")—for which [plugins](/wiki/Plug-in_(computing) "Plug-in (computing)"), by third parties, effect [virtual studio technology](/wiki/Virtual_Studio_Technology "Virtual Studio Technology").[[9]](#cite_note-Kadis-2013-9) DAWs fairly standard in the industry are Logic Pro and Pro Tools.[[10]](#cite_note-Joseph-2016-10) Physical devices involved include the main mixer, [MIDI](/wiki/MIDI "MIDI") controllers to communicate among equipment, the recording device itself, and perhaps effects gear that is outboard. Yet literal recording is sometimes still analog, onto tape, whereupon the raw recording is [converted to a digital signal](/wiki/Analog-to-digital_converter "Analog-to-digital converter") for processing and editing, as some producers still find audio advantages to recording onto tape.[[21]](#cite_note-Simmons-2006-21)

Conventionally, tape is more forgiving of [overmodulation](/wiki/Overmodulation "Overmodulation"), whereby dynamic peaks exceed the maximal recordable signal level: tape's limitation, a physical property, is magnetic capacity, which tapers off, smoothing the overmodulated waveform even at a signal nearly 15 decibels too "hot", whereas a digital recording is ruined by harsh distortion of "[clipping](/wiki/Clipping_(audio) "Clipping (audio)")" at any overshoot.[[21]](#cite_note-Simmons-2006-21) In digital recording, however, a recent advancement, [32-bit float](/wiki/32-bit_floating_point "32-bit floating point"), enables DAWs to undo clipping.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Still, some criticize digital instruments and workflows for excess automation, allegedly impairing creative or sonic control.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In any case, as production technology has drastically changed, so have the knowledge demands,[[24]](#cite_note-24) although DAWs enables novices, even teenagers at home, to learn production independently.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess2013199-11) Some have attained professional competence before ever working with an artist.[[12]](#cite_note-Newman-2018-12)

## Women in producing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Record_producer&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Women in producing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Audio_mixer_faders.jpg/250px-Audio_mixer_faders.jpg)](/wiki/File:Audio_mixer_faders.jpg)

Mixing console

Among female record producers, [Sylvia Moy](/wiki/Sylvia_Moy "Sylvia Moy") was the first at [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown"), [Gail Davies](/wiki/Gail_Davies "Gail Davies") the first on Nashville's [Music Row](/wiki/Music_Row "Music Row"), and [Ethel Gabriel](/wiki/Ethel_Gabriel "Ethel Gabriel"), with [RCA](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records"), the first at a [major record label](/wiki/Major_record_label "Major record label"). [Lillian McMurry](/wiki/Lillian_McMurry "Lillian McMurry"), owning [Trumpet Records](/wiki/Trumpet_Records "Trumpet Records"), produced influential [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") records. Meanwhile, [Wilma Cozart Fine](/wiki/Wilma_Cozart_Fine "Wilma Cozart Fine") produced hundreds of records for [Mercury Records](/wiki/Mercury_Records "Mercury Records")' classical division. For classical production, three women have won Grammy awards, and [Judith Sherman](/wiki/Judith_Sherman "Judith Sherman")'s 2015 win was her fifth.[[12]](#cite_note-Newman-2018-12) Yet in nonclassical, no woman has won [Producer of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Producer_of_the_Year,_Non-Classical "Grammy Award for Producer of the Year, Non-Classical"), awarded since 1975 and only one even nominated for a record not her own, Linda Perry. After [Lauren Christy](/wiki/Lauren_Christy "Lauren Christy")'s 2004 nomination, [Linda Perry](/wiki/Linda_Perry "Linda Perry")'s 2019 nomination was the next for a woman.[[25]](#cite_note-Leight-2018-25) On why no woman had ever won it, Perry commented, "I just don't think there are that many women interested."[[12]](#cite_note-Newman-2018-12) In the U.K., [Lynsey de Paul](/wiki/Lynsey_de_Paul "Lynsey de Paul") was an early female record producer, having produced both of her Ivor Novello award-winning songs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Across the decades, many female artists have produced their own music. For instance, artists [Kate Bush](/wiki/Kate_Bush "Kate Bush"), [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna"), [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey"), [Shakira](/wiki/Shakira "Shakira"), [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson"), [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") (even that of [Destiny's Child](/wiki/Destiny%27s_Child "Destiny's Child") and [the Carters](/wiki/The_Carters "The Carters")), [Lana Del Rey](/wiki/Lana_Del_Rey "Lana Del Rey"), [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift"), and [Lorde](/wiki/Lorde "Lorde") have produced or coproduced[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess2013199-11)[[26]](#cite_note-26) and [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande") who produces and arranges her vocals as well as being an audio engineer.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Still among specialists, despite some prominent women, including [Missy Elliott](/wiki/Missy_Elliott "Missy Elliott") in hip hop and [Sylvia Massy](/wiki/Sylvia_Massy "Sylvia Massy") in rock, the vast majority have been men.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess2013199-11) Early in the 2010s, asked for insights that she herself had gleaned as a woman who has specialized successfully in the industry, Wendy Page remarked, "The difficulties are usually very short-lived. Once people realize that you can do your job, sexism tends to lower its ugly head."[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess2013199-11) Still, when tasked to explain her profession's sex disparity, Page partly reasoned that record labels, dominated by men, have been, she said, "mistrustful of giving a woman the reins of an immense, creative project like making a record."[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess2013199-11) Ultimately, the reasons are multiple and not fully clear, although prominently proposed factors include types of sexism and scarcity of female role models in the profession.[[12]](#cite_note-Newman-2018-12)

In January 2018, a research team led by Stacy L. Smith, founder and director of the [Annenberg](/wiki/Annenberg_Foundation "Annenberg Foundation") Inclusion Initiative,[[30]](#cite_note-30) based in the [USC Annenberg School for Communication and Journalism](/wiki/USC_Annenberg_School_for_Communication_and_Journalism "USC Annenberg School for Communication and Journalism"),[[31]](#cite_note-Stereotyped-31) issued a report,[[32]](#cite_note-32) estimating that in the prior several years, about 2% of popular songs' producers were female.[[13]](#cite_note-Hertweck-2019-13) Also that month, *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine queried, "Where are all the female music producers?"[[12]](#cite_note-Newman-2018-12) Upon the Annenberg Inclusion Initiative's second annual report, released in February 2019,[[33]](#cite_note-33) its department at USC reported, "2018 saw an outcry from artists, executives and other music industry professionals over the lack of women in music" and "the plight of women in music", where women were allegedly being "stereotyped, sexualized, and shut out".[[31]](#cite_note-Stereotyped-31) Also in February 2019, the [Recording Academy](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy")'s Task Force on Diversity and Inclusion announced an initiative whereby over 200 artists and producers—ranging from [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B") and [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") to [Maroon 5](/wiki/Maroon_5 "Maroon 5") and [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones")—agreed to consider at least two women for each producer or engineer position.[[13]](#cite_note-Hertweck-2019-13) The academy's website, *Grammy.com*, announced, "This initiative is the first step in a broader effort to improve those numbers and increase diversity and inclusion for all in the music industry."[[13]](#cite_note-Hertweck-2019-13)
